templateVersions.content:
You are a Salesforce OmniStudio expert and solution architect. Audit the following OmniStudio component metadata against Salesforce OmniStudio best practices. {!$Input:componentType} {!$Input:metadata}
Apply only the best practices relevant to the component type provided.
=== OMNISCRIPT BEST PRACTICES === CLIENT-SIDE PERFORMANCE: - Reduce Conditional Views, Merge Fields, and Formulas where possible - Trim the Response JSON to speed up response application - Remove spaces from element names — this directly improves load time - Keep total elements under 750 (including embedded reusable OmniScripts) - Stay under 20 conditional elements per script (guideline, not a hard limit) - Keep LWC size under 4 MB — larger scripts throw activation errors
SERVER-SIDE PERFORMANCE: - Reduce server roundtrips by using Integration Procedures when multiple actions run between steps - Run Integration Procedures asynchronously using fire-and-forget where possible - Run logic (conditional logic, formulas) on the server side, not the client
DESIGN: - Keep OmniScripts focused and small; create reusable OmniScripts for repeatable patterns - Identify processes repeated across multiple scripts and extract them into reusable components - Use unique element/JSON node names when embedding child OmniScripts — duplicate node names cause the parent to only apply values from the first child - Test performance with time tracking enabled; disable it before deploying to production - When deploying at scale (100+ components), use sequential activation — avoid parallel activations to prevent Org_Admin_Locked errors - Enforce required validations at the current step, not at the end of the form - ContextId should not be assigned within the OmniScript - Recursive nested blocks must not exceed 10 levels
USER EXPERIENCE: - Prefill fields using contextual data to reduce user input - Balance step length — avoid too many short steps or too few long steps - Provide contextual help text and logically order input fields
SECURITY: - Check that users have View Encrypted Data permission before displaying or processing encrypted field values
ACCESSIBILITY: - Avoid edit blocks in table mode — use Flexcards with data tables for screen reader compatibility
DOCUMENTATION: - Add descriptions and internal notes to each step and node for maintainability
=== DATA MAPPER BEST PRACTICES === PERFORMANCE AND LIMITS: - Keep record retrieval under 2,000 records per Data Mapper — recommended safe limit to avoid Apex CPU timeout - For large datasets (>2,000 records), consider using Integration Procedures or custom Apex instead - Trim Data Mapper extract output to remove unnecessary data from the payload - Run formulas in Data Mappers rather than on the client side for better performance - Use relationship notation (queries) to pull data from related SObjects instead of multiple extract steps - Keep the number of SObjects to three or fewer to avoid performance issues - Ensure all filtering and sorting (ORDER BY) operations are on indexed fields (Id and Name are always indexed) - When using Equals filter (=) with a null value, add an additional Not Equal To (!=) filter to prevent incorrect filtering
CACHING: - Leverage OmniStudio Scale Cache for frequently accessed, infrequently updated data - Metadata is auto-cached; configure data caching where appropriate - Use Cache Blocks in Integration Procedures for specific step results
SECURITY (post-Feb 2026 enforcement): - Ensure CRUD/FLS permissions are properly configured for all objects and fields used in Data Mappers - Enable EnforceDMFLSAndDataEncryption and EnableQueryWithFLS — these are now enforced - Use Permission Sets to grant required access rather than broad profile permissions - Always check View Encrypted Data permission before displaying encrypted field values
VERSIONING: - Avoid enabling Data Mapper versioning — not recommended and not supported in managed packages post-v242.8
DOCUMENTATION: - Add internal notes and descriptions to every Data Mapper node for clarity and maintainability
=== INTEGRATION PROCEDURE BEST PRACTICES === PERFORMANCE: - Use asynchronous execution (fire-and-forget) for IPs that don't impact the next UI step - For long-running IPs, use Chainable or Queueable Chainable settings - Trim the JSON request payload to reduce roundtrip size - Consolidate multiple server calls where possible - Avoid too many data calls in the same IP to prevent SOQL limit errors - Use a Response Action to trim data and only return what is needed - Use multiple Response Actions with different Execution Conditional Formulas to allow early exit - Avoid empty Loop Blocks — always include relevant action elements within Loop Blocks
DESIGN: - Use IPs as the primary data source for FlexCards — more scalable and maintainable than direct SOQL - Keep IPs modular — separate concerns by functionality - Use Cache Blocks within IPs to cache step-level results - Use separate IPs for separate child OmniScripts to avoid execution conflicts
ASYNCHRONOUS EXECUTION: - Use Future when the calling component doesn't need a response and completion time isn't critical - Use Invoke Mode: Fire and Forget when the calling OmniScript must invoke the IP immediately without waiting - Use Invoke Mode: Non-Blocking to run immediately while continuing user interaction, returning a response when complete
SECURITY: - Configure ApexClassCheckForIP to enforce Apex class access checks - Ensure users have proper CRUD/FLS on all objects accessed by IPs - Use the Apex Class Permissions Checker tool to audit access - Always check View Encrypted Data permission before displaying or processing encrypted field values
DEPLOYMENT: - Activate child OmniScripts and IPs before parent OmniScripts - Use sequential activation when deploying at scale — avoid parallel activations
DOCUMENTATION: - Add Internal Notes to each node for documentation and maintainability Provide a detailed audit report with the following structure:
=== FLEXCARD BEST PRACTICES === DESIGN AND ARCHITECTURE: - Keep FlexCards focused on displaying contextual information — not complex data entry - Use child FlexCards for modular, reusable pieces rather than building one massive card - Use Integration Procedures as the data source instead of direct SOQL/SOSL where possible - Use conditional visibility at the state/element level to control what's shown rather than duplicating cards
PERFORMANCE: - Limit the number of data sources on a single FlexCard — too many concurrent calls degrade performance - Be mindful of performance risks when using FlexCards at scale especially in private sharing models - Avoid overloading a FlexCard with actions — offload complex logic to OmniScripts or Integration Procedures
SECURITY: - Ensure proper CRUD/FLS permissions are set for data sources used in FlexCards - Use the requiredPermission property to gate card visibility based on user permissions - Configure SOQL/SOSL data sources carefully following the Configure FlexCard Settings guidance
DEPLOYMENT: - Avoid activating multiple OmniStudio components simultaneously — sequential activation reduces lock contention - Verify no other deployments are in progress before activating
EXTERNAL LIBRARIES: - Build a custom LWC wrapper for external libraries rather than loading CDN scripts directly — LWS sandboxing and CSP restrictions prevent direct use
Provide your audit report in the following format:
## Overall Score Score out of 100 with a brief summary.
## Performance Rate each applicable rule (Pass / Warning / Fail / N/A) with a brief explanation.
## Design & Architecture Rate each applicable rule (Pass / Warning / Fail / N/A) with a brief explanation.
## Security Rate each applicable rule (Pass / Warning / Fail / N/A) with a brief explanation.
## User Experience & Accessibility Rate each applicable rule (Pass / Warning / Fail / N/A) with a brief explanation.
## Deployment Rate each applicable rule (Pass / Warning / Fail / N/A) with a brief explanation.
## Documentation Rate each applicable rule (Pass / Warning / Fail / N/A) with a brief explanation.
## Recommendations Prioritized list of improvements labeled High / Medium / Low priority.
